statement: On 20 October 2012, Iceland held a six-question advisory referendum on constitutional reform proposals drafted by the Stjórnlagaráð (Constitutional Council). The first and most significant question — 'Do you wish the proposals of the Constitutional Council to form the basis of a new Constitution?' — received 66.9% yes votes (49,486 yes vs 24,521 no). All six questions received majority yes votes. Turnout was 48.9% (115,890 of approximately 237,000 eligible voters), significantly lower than typical parliamentary election turnout of 80–85%. The referendum was advisory (ráðgefandi), not legally binding, as the Icelandic constitution does not provide for binding referendums on policy questions. Despite the clear majority in favour, no subsequent Alþingi has enacted the draft into law.
source_name: Landskjörstjórn (National Electoral Commission) — Official results of the 20 October 2012 referendum
caveats: The advisory nature of the referendum means Alþingi was under no legal obligation to act on the results. Critics of inaction argue the democratic mandate was clear; defenders of inaction cite the low turnout and argue that constitutional change requires broader consensus. The six questions were also criticised for being leading or bundled.

related_entries.statement: Iceland's constitution (Stjórnarskrá lýðveldisins Íslands) contains no explicit provision on transfer of sovereignty to international organisations. Legal scholars have argued that EU accession would require a constitutional amendment under Article 79, which mandates that amendments be passed by the Alþingi, followed by dissolution, a general election, and passage again by the new Alþingi. The 2026 referendum is advisory (ráðgefandi þjóðaratkvæðagreiðsla), not binding, as the constitution does not provide for binding referendums on policy questions (Article 26 covers only presidential referral of legislation).

EU accession would require constitutional change in Iceland. Article 2 of the Icelandic Constitution (Stjórnarskrá) vests legislative power jointly in the Althingi and the President, and Article 21 provides that the President can refuse to sign legislation. There is no explicit provision for transferring sovereignty to international organisations. Legal scholars are divided on whether accession would require a constitutional amendment — some argue that a simple act of parliament ratifying the accession treaty would suffice, while others insist that the transfer of legislative competence to EU institutions requires amending Article 2. The 2024 referendum act specifies that the result is binding, but does not address the constitutional amendment question.

citations.report_title: Treystu þjóðinni — Opið bréf til forsætisráðherra Íslands

claims.claim_text: Í þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunni 2012 um nýja stjórnarskrá samþykktu yfir 2/3 hlutar kjósenda (67%) að tillögurnar skyldu verða grundvöllur nýrrar stjórnarskrár Íslands.
Alþingi hefur ekki enn virt úrslit þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunnar 2012 um nýja stjórnarskrá.
Í tillögunum að nýrri stjórnarskrá var kveðið á um rétt þjóðarinnar yfir sameiginlegum auðlindum lands og sjávar, og það var samþykkt í þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu.
